title: Singularity formation for some incompressible Euler flows
speaker: Tarek Elgindi
abstract: We describe a recent construction of self-similar blow-up solutions of the incompressible Euler equation. A consequence of the construction is that there exist finite-energy $C^{1,a}$ solutions to the Euler equation which develop a singularity in finite time for some range of $a>0$. The approach we follow is to isolate a simple non-linear equation which encodes the leading order dynamics of solutions to the Euler equation in some regimes and then prove that the simple equation has stable self-similar blow-up solutions.

proof_stages.label: Euler setup, blow-up criterion, and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up incompressible Euler, recalls energy conservation, C^{1,α} local well-posedness and the Beale-Kato-Majda vorticity criterion, then states the main result: for very small α there is C^{1,α} data with decaying gradient whose local solution blows up self-similarly at t=1.

proof_stages.label: Singular-regular decomposition of the vorticity-to-velocity map
proof_stages.summary: The Biot-Savart/Riesz operator recovering ∇u from vorticity is decomposed into a well-understood 1/α singular radial part coming from the second angular mode and an O(1) regular remainder, yielding the schematic form ∇u=(1/α)L(ω)+O(1).

proof_stages.label: Mechanism favoring vortex stretching over transport
proof_stages.summary: A one-dimensional analog of the vorticity equation is used to explain that vortex stretching drives growth whereas transport can deplete it, so blow-up scenarios should make transport weaker than stretching.

proof_stages.label: Self-similar alpha-zero model in axisymmetric no-swirl symmetry
proof_stages.summary: Using the singular part of ∇u, the argument restricts to axisymmetric no-swirl flows and studies the α≈0 reduced equation, whose radial operator permits many explicit self-similar profiles that serve as base blow-up solutions.

proof_stages.label: Positive-alpha perturbative ansatz and modulation parameters
proof_stages.summary: For the full positive-α equation, the speaker searches for a self-similar profile F=F0+g, with extra parameters μ and λ introduced to enforce the necessary low-mode solvability conditions; the same structure is illustrated through a simpler perturbative model equation.

proof_stages.label: Coercive linearization and closure of the perturbation
proof_stages.summary: Linearizing about the base profile gives an operator with a coercive estimate in weighted Sobolev spaces; choosing μ and λ cancels the quadratic vanishing obstruction at z=0, leading to an a priori bound ∥g∥≤Cε and hence existence of the perturbed Euler blow-up profile.

examples.name: The function xy log(x^2+y^2) as a Riesz-transform counterexample
examples.note: This classical two-dimensional function has bounded Laplacian but unbounded second derivatives, and is presented as the bad mode that motivates isolating the singular part of the Biot-Savart operator.
examples.engagement_reason: he names the function as the standard counterexample and states its Laplacian is bounded while a second derivative is unbounded, without deriving this on the function

examples.name: Hölder second-angular-mode profile
examples.note: The profile xy/(x^2+y^2)^{1-alpha}, equivalently a sin(2θ) r^α-type mode, makes concrete which Hölder angular component creates the 1/α singular contribution.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the Riesz/Laplace-inverse analysis to the second angular mode, noting the theorem fails for sin(2θ)r^α and computing its inverse as a 1/α radial part plus order-one terms

passes.reason: The homogeneous Hölder n=2 angular mode is the resonant excluded profile causing the 1/α singular term and obstructing an α-uniform estimate without orthogonality.
The Hölder n=2 angular Fourier profile is the critical resonant mode producing the 1/alpha singular term when the orthogonality condition is absent.
the Hölder r^α sin(2θ) profile is the critical n=2 obstruction showing the angular orthogonality condition is necessary.
The pure Hölder n=2 angular mode is the critical bad mode producing the 1/alpha singular part and obstructing an alpha-uniform estimate without orthogonality.
The pure Hölder n=2 angular mode is the resonant critical mode excluded by the orthogonality condition and obstructs a uniform alpha-independent bound.
The pure Hölder n=2 angular profile is the resonant mode producing the 1/alpha loss, obstructing the alpha-uniform estimate without orthogonality.
The Hölder pure second angular mode is the resonant excluded enemy profile that produces the sharp 1/alpha singular part of the transform.
The Hölder profile r^alpha sin(2theta) is the resonant second angular mode producing the 1/alpha singular term, obstructing a uniform estimate without mode-2 orthogonality.
The Hölder r^α sin(2θ) mode is the bad resonant angular profile producing the 1/α singular term, showing the orthogonality condition is necessary.
The r^alpha sin(2theta) Hölder mode is the resonant bad mode showing the alpha-independent estimate cannot include the second angular component.
The pure second angular Hölder mode is the resonant obstruction to the uniform estimate without the orthogonality condition, saturating the critical angular-mode behavior.
the Hölder profile r^alpha sin(2theta) is the resonant second angular mode producing the 1/alpha singular part, showing uniform estimates fail without the orthogonality condition.

examples.name: Sine versus sine-to-the-alpha data in the one-dimensional model
examples.note: The stationary solution omega=sin x illustrates exact transport/stretching balance, while omega≈sin^α x for α<1 illustrates how a cusp weakens transport and allows stretching-driven blow-up.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through the 1D model for sin x versus sin^α x data, checking the transport-stretching balance, stationarity of sin x, and why the Hölder profile favors blow-up

passes.reason: The sine profile is the critical balanced stationary case while the sublinear sine^α data gives blow-up below the C1 threshold.
The sine-to-the-alpha datum has a non-C1 Hölder cusp and is used to show global regularity fails below the linear-at-the-origin threshold, unlike the stationary sine datum.
the comparison uses the critical linear sine profile and a sub-C1 Hölder cusp, with sine serving as a stationary obstruction to the naive stretching-growth heuristic.
The sine-to-the-alpha cusp data lie at the rough side of the C1 threshold and are contrasted with the stationary sine data to show finite-time blowup can occur in the model.
The contrast puts linear sine data at the exact transport-stretching balance while sine-to-the-alpha data lies below the C1 threshold and gives blow-up.
The comparison sits at the model’s critical C1 regularity threshold between the steady linear sine profile and blow-up Hölder cusp data.
The sine-to-the-alpha datum is used against global regularity below the C1 threshold, while sine marks the critical stationary linear-vanishing case.
The sine-to-the-alpha datum is used as the bad low-regularity blow-up case, contrasted with sin x as the critical balanced stationary alpha=1 profile.
The sine-to-the-α data is used as a low-regularity blow-up case against global regularity below C¹, while the sine profile marks the exact balance threshold.
The sine versus sine^alpha data illustrate the critical C^1/Hölder threshold between exact transport-stretching balance and blow-up in the model.
The comparison uses the stationary sine profile and the cusp-like sine-to-the-alpha data to show the smooth balance/global-regularity intuition fails below the C1 threshold.
sin x is the critical stationary balance while sin^alpha x below the C1 threshold blows up, exhibiting failure of global regularity at Hölder regularity.

examples.name: Dirichlet-boundary reflection picture
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the boundary case by reflecting data across the boundary, showing pictorially how the boundary can help push nonzero vorticity into the bulk.
examples.engagement_reason: he explains the Dirichlet case by carrying out the reflection picture and tracing how reflected boundary vorticity is pushed outward and helps blow-up

passes.reason: The Dirichlet construction is an exact reflected extension across the boundary used to explain the boundary mechanism rather than to refute a claim.
